ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'coffee beverage' class; no specific Vietnamese phin-brewed black coffee class exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers black coffee as a beverage category; no Vietnamese-specific phin-brewed variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 refers to coffee as a substance; used as the closest defensible anchor for this beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one cup or glass of Vietnamese black coffee brewed through a phin filter, served hot or over ice.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 171890
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Vietnamese phin-brewed black coffee is not listed as a distinct entry in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for brewed black coffee (FDC ID 171890) is used as the closest approved-source equivalent. Vietnamese phin brewing produces a stronger concentrate than standard drip coffee; however, when served as a full cup diluted to approximately 250 ml, the per-100 ml nutrient profile is comparable to standard brewed black coffee. Input per_100g values from the original record (energy 2 kcal, protein 0.12 g, fat 0.04 g, carbohydrate 0.36 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy brewed coffee values and are retained. Sodium value of 2 mg/100 g is consistent with USDA data. Potassium (49 mg), riboflavin (0.076 mg), niacin (0.191 mg), phosphorus (3 mg), magnesium (3 mg), and water (99.4 g) are drawn from USDA FDC ID 171890.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; reference: Atkinson FS et al., International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain unsweetened black coffee contains negligible digestible carbohydrates (0.36 g/100 ml). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) confirm that unsweetened black coffee does not raise blood glucose and is not assigned a GI value. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' per Veritas scoring rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (null GI × 0.36 g carb × 2.5) / 100 = 0.0 by convention.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (2 mg/100 ml); no added sugar, cream, or salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain Vietnamese black coffee contains only 2 mg sodium per 100 ml and no added sweeteners or creamers, making it acceptable for hypertensive individuals when limited to 2–3 cups per day to avoid acute caffeine-driven transient blood pressure elevation; do not add sugar or condensed milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero added sugar; negligible carbohydrate (0.36 g/100 ml); chlorogenic acids may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened Vietnamese black coffee contains no added sugar and negligible carbohydrate, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; epidemiological evidence suggests chlorogenic acids in robusta coffee may improve insulin sensitivity over time, but individuals should avoid adding sugar or condensed milk and monitor for caffeine-related glucose variability.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero trans fat, negligible sodium; moderate habitual coffee consumption associated with reduced CVD risk in meta-analyses
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain Vietnamese black coffee contains no saturated fat, no trans fat, and negligible sodium, and moderate consumption (2–3 cups per day) is associated with reduced cardiovascular disease risk in large meta-analyses; individuals with arrhythmia or caffeine sensitivity should limit intake and avoid adding cream or sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chlorogenic acids and polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory properties; no pro-inflammatory ingredients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain Vietnamese black coffee contains chlorogenic acids and polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties and no pro-inflammatory ingredients (zero trans fat, zero saturated fat, zero added sugar); limit to 2–3 cups per day as excessive caffeine may interfere with sleep and worsen RA symptom perception.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vietnamese black coffee (Cà phê đen đá / nóng) is a traditional Vietnamese beverage made by slowly dripping hot water through finely ground robusta coffee beans packed into a small metal phin filter placed directly over a cup. The result is a concentrated, intensely flavoured black coffee that is served either hot (nóng) directly from the filter or poured over a glass of ice (đá) to produce a refreshing iced version. Robusta beans, which are the dominant coffee variety grown in Vietnam, impart a bold, slightly bitter, and earthy flavour with higher caffeine content than arabica-based coffees. The beverage contains no milk, sugar, or additives in its plain form, making it vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Nutritionally, plain Vietnamese black coffee is extremely low in calories (approximately 2 kcal per 100 ml), contains negligible protein (0.12 g/100 ml), minimal fat (0.04 g/100 ml), and a small amount of carbohydrate (0.36 g/100 ml) with zero added sugar. Sodium content is negligible at 2 mg/100 ml. The beverage has negligible glycemic impact and does not raise blood glucose, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or condensed milk. Chlorogenic acids and polyphenols present in robusta coffee have been associated with anti-inflammatory effects and potential improvements in insulin sensitivity. A standard serving is approximately 250 ml. This coffee is a cultural staple in Vietnam and is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants and cafes across Southeast Asia and Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is Vietnamese black coffee suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Vietnamese black coffee?
Can I drink black coffee if I have hypertension?
Vietnamese coffee without condensed milk calories

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese phin-brewed black coffee exists in any approved primary regional source. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brewed black coffee (FDC ID 171890) is used as the closest approved-source equivalent and is consistent with the input record values. The per-100 ml nutrient profile of phin-brewed black coffee served at standard dilution is comparable to standard brewed black coffee. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact brewing concentration of Vietnamese phin coffee may vary and no approved source has a dedicated Vietnamese black coffee entry.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171890, 'Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water') as the closest approved-source equivalent; no dedicated Vietnamese phin-brewed black coffee entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Input record values are consistent with USDA data and have been retained with minor supplementation from USDA FDC ID 171890. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for brewed black coffee is not reported as a primary value in the USDA SR Legacy entry for FDC ID 171890 at the per-100 ml level relevant to this beverage. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are either not reported or reported as zero/trace in USDA FoodData Central for brewed black coffee and are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific beverage. 4. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171890 for brewed black coffee. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero in plain brewed coffee with no added fats. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero as no sugar is added to plain black coffee. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for brewed black coffee (soluble fibre passes through filter). cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero in a plant-derived beverage with no dairy. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171890. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in a plant-derived beverage. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in a plant-derived beverage. 5. glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable': plain unsweetened black coffee contains negligible carbohydrates (0.36 g/100 ml), making GI measurement not applicable per Veritas scoring rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by convention. Reference: Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care; University of Sydney GI Database. 6. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam as the dish is a Vietnamese cultural staple. The record was sourced from a Singapore-based GrabFood listing. 7. Ontology codes are defensible closest-parent matches; no exact Vietnamese phin coffee class exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT as of 2026. Gap notes provided for all three ontology systems.
